message.content:
You are confirmer #1, independent. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (fully closes it; for a code change the named regression test must DISCRIMINATE — fail if the fix were reverted). Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix G-022 in .github/workflows/ci.yml
What: pip-audit==2.10.1 and pip-licenses==5.5.5 version-pinned for reproducible supply-chain gates.
Test(s): test_audit_tooling_is_version_pinned
Steps: read the actual changed file(s); read the test and judge whether it discriminates; check no regression. For a doc/refactor item, judge correctness/coverage rather than revert-discrimination. Verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN (something missing/wrong/under-tested). Be skeptical and specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.description: Run the regression test with python3

content.text: Let me verify discrimination logically by checking the test assertions against the file, and confirm whether the assertions would fail on revert. The test checks for `pip-audit==` and `pip-licenses==` substrings. Let me confirm both exist and check the negative-assertion guards.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && echo "== pip-audit== present? ==" && grep -c "pip-audit==" .github/workflows/ci.yml && echo "== pip-licenses== present? ==" && grep -c "pip-licenses==" .github/workflows/ci.yml && echo "== negative guard string present (should be 0)? ==" && grep -c "install --upgrade pip pip-audit" .github/workflows/ci.yml
input.description: Confirm pinned substrings and negative guard

content.content:
== pip-audit== present? ==
1
== pip-licenses== present? ==
1
== negative guard string present (should be 0)? ==
0

content.text: Now let me confirm the test would discriminate on a revert. A pre-fix unpinned version would be `pip install ... pip-audit` (no `==`) and `pip install pip-licenses` (no `==`). The positive assertions `pip-audit==` and `pip-licenses==` would both fail under revert. Let me simulate.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && cp .github/workflows/ci.yml /tmp/ci.bak && sed -i '' 's/pip-audit==2.10.1/pip-audit/; s/pip-licenses==5.5.5/pip-licenses/' .github/workflows/ci.yml && echo "after revert-sim:" && grep -n "pip-audit\b\|pip-licenses\b" .github/workflows/ci.yml | grep -v "==" | grep "pip install\|pip-licenses --\|pip-licenses=="; echo "---grep for =="; grep -c "pip-audit==\|pip-licenses==" .github/workflows/ci.yml; mv /tmp/ci.bak .github/workflows/ci.yml && echo "restored"
input.description: Simulate revert and check assertions, then restore

content.content:
after revert-sim:
101:        run: python -m pip install --upgrade pip && python -m pip install pip-audit
170:          python -m pip install pip-licenses  # pinned for reproducible license output (#G-022)
171:          pip-licenses --format=json --with-system > licenses.json
---grep for ==

content.text:
Confirmed. Under a revert (unpinned), `grep -c "pip-audit==\|pip-licenses=="` returns 0, so both positive assertions (`assert "pip-audit==" in text` and `assert "pip-licenses==" in text`) would fail. The test discriminates.

Let me verify the file is restored cleanly and check that pip-audit usage in the SBOM step (line 151) and gate (131) still reference the installed pinned tool — no separate unpinned install path.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git diff --stat .github/workflows/ci.yml; echo "---all pip install of audit tools---"; grep -n "pip install.*pip-audit\|pip install.*pip-licenses\|pip-licenses==" .github/workflows/ci.yml
input.description: Confirm clean restore and all install paths pinned

content.content:
---all pip install of audit tools---
101:        run: python -m pip install --upgrade pip && python -m pip install pip-audit==2.10.1
170:          python -m pip install pip-licenses==5.5.5  # pinned for reproducible license output (#G-022)

content.text:
File restored clean (no diff). Both install paths are the only ones, both pinned. No regression: `pip install --upgrade pip` is still unpinned but that's pip itself (the tooling-version concern G-022 is specifically pip-audit/pip-licenses, and pip is the package manager being upgraded, consistent with prior practice). 

Assessment:
- Appropriate: pip-audit==2.10.1 and pip-licenses==5.5.5 pinned at the two (and only two) install sites; idiomatic exact-pin matching the existing uv pin convention (#F-031); no other install path bypasses the pin; no regression.
- Complete: test asserts both pinned substrings present AND guards against the old unpinned `install --upgrade pip pip-audit` pattern. Revert simulation showed the positive assertions go to 0 matches, so the test fails on revert — it discriminates.

One minor note: the negative guard at line 261 (`"install --upgrade pip pip-audit\n"`) doesn't exactly match the actual pre-fix string (the install was `--upgrade pip && ... pip-audit`), so that specific guard is somewhat vestigial — but the two positive `==` assertions fully carry the discrimination, so completeness holds.

input.reasoning:
Fix is appropriate and complete.

APPROPRIATE: In /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml the audit tooling is pinned at the only two install sites: line 101 `python -m pip install pip-audit==2.10.1` and line 170 `python -m pip install pip-licenses==5.5.5`. grep confirms exactly one `pip-audit==` and one `pip-licenses==`, and zero remaining unpinned `pip install ... pip-audit/pip-licenses` paths. Exact-pin is idiomatic and mirrors the existing uv pin convention (#F-031). The SBOM/gate steps (lines 131,151,171) invoke the already-pinned installed binaries — no bypass. No regression (the `pip install --upgrade pip` upgrade is pip itself, not the audit tooling).

COMPLETE / DISCRIMINATES: test_audit_tooling_is_version_pinned (tests/test_supply_chain.py:255) reads the actual ci.yml via _ci_text() and asserts both `pip-audit==` and `pip-licenses==` are present. I simulated a revert with sed (strip the version pins): `grep -c "pip-audit==|pip-licenses=="` dropped to 0, so both positive assertions would fail — the test fails if the fix is reverted. File restored, git diff clean.

Minor: the line-261 negative guard string `install --upgrade pip pip-audit\n` does not exactly match the real pre-fix string (`--upgrade pip && ... pip-audit`), so that one guard is vestigial, but the two positive `==` assertions fully carry discrimination. Not blocking.

content.text: Verdict: CONFIRMED. The G-022 fix pins both pip-audit==2.10.1 and pip-licenses==5.5.5 at the only two install sites in ci.yml, and the regression test discriminates (revert simulation drops both pinned substrings to zero matches, failing the test).
